content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Tremors (1990) is such a fun and highly entertaining movie.

**Score:** 203
**Body:** • Chemistry between all of the cast​ is outstanding. The movie has a lot of funny  moments and dialogues.

• Graboids are genuinely scary, pretty fast , and decently smart too. I like the monsters very much.

• There are also several exhilarating moments, like roof top scene and escape scene.

• I loved the slightly mad couple who literally had fuckton tools and and weapons.

• The soundtrack is also great and movie doesn't leave any wasting space.

**Imo, its a solid 10/10 watch.**  ​

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tremors is a lot of fun. I'm adding that to my rewatch list. Probably haven't seen it for fifteen years? 
  - It holds up so well the practical effects and that small town vibe just hit different you will have a blast on a rewatch
2. I work from home in my basement. A few years ago we had a "powerful for NJ" earthquake. I immediately joked it's the Tremors monsters. We watched 1, 2, and 3 that weekend.
3. When I was a kid sometimes I would start thinking of tremors when I was on my way across the yard or getting out of the car and I would have to make a mad dash for the house. I was so scared lol.
4. What kind of fuse is that?

Cannon fuse.

What the hell do you use it for?

My cannon.
5. Love. It's a great creature horror that I never hear about
6. Saw it in the theater as a teenager. It's a B movie that is actually prestige.
7. We’re not handymen. We’re handy men. RIP Fred Ward.
8. Some people describe it as a “perfect” movie.

Every scene sets up something that pays off later. No line of dialogue is wasted. Practical effects are stellar. Even the opening foreshadows the climax at the end. Incredibly tight script.

A good example is Chang’s refrigeration pump being broken, and how that pays off later because the main characters hadn’t fixed it yet.

9. The dude‎, Michael Gross, with the weapons was from Family Ties, his character was literally a hippie who had changed with the times, so him being a gun nut goes against type. The wife was famous as well, not sure what for. 

## 2. The Arrival with Charlie Sheen

**Score:** 55
**Body:** I'm posting this because the other day someone posted asking for the best scifi movies, and I suggested this, and I got down voted. And that annoys me lol. This movie is awesome. Is it a little 90s campy? Yes it is but it's still excellent. One of my favorite Alien movies of all time. And Sheen is perfect in it. I don't think any other actor nails that serious but a little ridiculous vibe that he had. Anyway if you are looking for a good scifi movie you can do MUCH worse than this one 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The production of this movie bought my father's 1968 Saab station wagon to serve as Charlie Sheen's car. They painted it green and had Sheen's character crash it through a gate. They probably had multiple versions, but my dad's car was one of them.
  - That's awesome
  - I almost downvoted because you did not begin your post with "Let me tell you a Saab story"
2. Not my favorite SF movie but yeah, it's a good one.

Any other suggestions OP?
  - Fire in the Sky 
3. It's one of those movies that didn't seem like it had any right to be as good as it was. Not a masterpiece but genuinely a solid film. 
4. I thought it was great.  Very mission impossible style movie
5. Very good movie.
6. I saw this as a free Movie on YouTube, just randomly picked it and started watching one night. Thought it was great. I like how it ties in a climate change conspiracy too. And Aliens.
7. That part about the 'genetic dice' sticks with me 30 years later lol. It was good as a kid, I liked it

## 3. I’ve basically watched no movies

**Score:** 27
**Body:** I am far too old to have only seen random romcoms and chick flicks. I recently watched fight club for the first time and realised I’ve been missing out heavily. Please recommend any and all movies that you think everyone should see at least once so I can stop being so damn uncultured. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Goodfellas

The Thing (1982)

Once upon a Time in Hollywood

Saving Private Ryan

Jaws

Jurassic Park

12 Angry Men

Dumb and Dumber

Spirited Away
  - That's gotta be the most random, incomprehensible list I've ever seen. 

Having said that. I love every movie on this list.
2. Galaxy Quest.
3. The Wizard of Oz

Casablanca

Lawrence of Arabia

You'll get heaps of other great suggestions here, but those three pop to mind immediately as universal classics that still have impact in modern film. WoO for the design and storytelling, Casablanca for the absolutely iconic dialog, LoA for the astonishing cinematography. 
4. Princess Bride

Monty Python and the Holy Grail

The Godfather

Little Miss Sunshine 

Shawshank Redemption 

The Green Mile

Back to the Future

Gone with the Wind

The Wizard of Oz

Toy Story
5. i'm not going to give you specific films, but I would strongly suggest you take a look at Academy Award winners and nominees in the following categories:

\* Best Picture  
\* Best Director  
\* Best Screenplay (Original and Adapted)   
\* Best Lead performances and supporting (though the film itself may be meh)   
\* Best Film Editing - these tend to be quality movies as well, as the pacing of the editing is largely dictated by the script and the director. 

I would also suggest you look at films that perform well at the box office year over year.

Furthermore, subscribe to Letterbox'd. You can log movies you've seen, but also find lists by other users. 

As you discover, you'll learn about the style of films you're drawn to. 

I'm so happy you've discovered a fondness for movies. It's the best art form. 
6. Jaws, Lord Of the Rings trilogy, and the Matrix is a good start.
  - Only the first Matrix though.
7. Chinatown
8. Stand By Me

The Shawshank Redemption

A Nightmare on Elm Street

The Breakfast Club

Home Alone

The Matrix 

Jurassic Park

Terminator 1+2

Scream 

Mary and Max 

Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles ''90

Falling Down 

Bloodsport 

Fright Night 



## 4. Looking for movies with "breathtaking" cinematography and excellent music

**Score:** 24
**Body:** I recently watched Pride & Prejudice (2005) and absolutely loved it — the cinematography was stunning and the music was just beautiful. The combination of those sweeping landscapes and classical score really elevated the whole experience for me.
Now I’m looking for similar movies. They don’t necessarily have to be romantic — genre doesn’t matter much.

Basically, films where the visuals and music work together in a powerful way.
Would love your recommendations! 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lawrence of Arabia
  - This \^\^
  - Hard to disagree with this one
2. Chariots of Fire (1981) won 4 Academy Awards including best picture and best score. Set in Britain in 1920s.
3. Out of Africa
4. Last of the Mohicans (1992ish)
5. Days of Heaven, Thin Red Line, Stalker, Blade Runner, Baraka
6. Phantom Thread
7. Barry Lyndon


8. *Soy Cuba / I Am Cuba* (1964)   —    Directed by Mikhail Kalatozov

## 5. Movies with strong female leads that aren't explicitly dramas/romance.

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Just finished watching Send Help with Rachel McAdams and my partner loved it and was complaining about how we never see movies with strong lead women, particularly in blockbusters or where they don't need saving by a "strong man".

I've already got Alien tee'd up to show her as the quintessential strong female lead but would love other movies in a similar vein. The more fun the better probably...

Can be anything from action, thriller, dark comedies (like Send Help), even Everything Everywhere All At Once got her teary eyed and she enjoyed it. I'll take it all!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fargo (gotta love Margie)
2. Drag me to Hell

GI Jane

Mad Max: Fury Road
3. Edge of Tomorrow (Emily Blunt is a delight)

Sicario (Emily Blunt ...again)

Silence of the Lambs

Everything, Everywhere, All at Once 

Atomic Blonde

Ready or Not 

Ocean's 8
4. Peppermint

Salt

Hannah
5. Aliens, terminator 2.
6. Let the right one in (2008)
7. PREY (such a great film, the Predator vs Alien also has a badass female lead)

May

American Mary

Promising Young Woman

The Beguiled

Maleficent

The Long Kiss Goodnight

The Edge of Tomorrow

Harley Quinn/Birds Of Prey

Star Wars (princess Leia)

Kill Bill

Tomb Raider

Linda Hamilton in Terminator 2
8. serial mom starring kathleen turner
9. Three billboards outside ebbing Missouri is my favorite strong female lead movie.
10. Arrival (amazing sci fi movie) 

Contact (another sci fi. Jodie Foster classic)

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. This whole scene in Hot Fuzz always cracks me up, and overall it’s a pretty fun watch. What’s your opinion on Hot Fuzz?

**Score:** 2239

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|FbD2TB4CIYH2U)
2. Great movie. Sharp script. Incredible pacing. Wonderfully acted. Bloody as hell. What more could you ask for?
3. https://preview.redd.it/bdc79osh7glg1.jpeg?width=346&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=a2eda63cdef97f8e273cb316867b69b0fe19821e
4. The Romeo and Juliet play is still, I think, the funniest thing Edgar Wright has ever done.

That and “I’m a mad slasher…OF PRICES”
  - The only victim here was the works of William Shakespear
5. I really enjoy it.
Probably my favorite from the Three flavors Cornetto Trilogy.

Love the twist, great writing, and fantastic cast
6. "Everyone and their mums is packin' 'round here"

I loved the Andys 😂
  - "Like who?"

"...farmers..."

"Who else?"

"...farmer's mums..."
7. ![gif](giphy|111T19fGfB7k1W|downsized)
8. Great movie, fantastic pacing, and I love Timothy Dalton in it.

## 2. Huge if true

**Score:** 605

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|xOD2M0aHX5hjIbUbq9)


2. Heat 2: More Heat
  - Heat 2: Heater
    - To heat to handle
3. Don’t do it….leave it alone
  - If they must touch it, this isn’t the worst cast.
    - True but even Denzel Washington couldn’t save Gladiator 2. Ill hope for the best but I agree with the above comment to leave it alone
  - You know, I came to brigade against the sequel and I wrote a few lines to express my displeasure but... It's Michael Mann's movie. He wrote the OG, he directed the OG and if he wants to do a sequel, so be it.     
I LOVE Michael Mann movies. AIl of them, so I'm gonna see what he does here.      
Yes, the OG is perfection
4. 1995 we got Pacino and De Niro. Two icons, heavyweights, the greatest of their generation (and in my opinion of all time) finally on screen together

2027? We will get DiCaprio and Bale. In my opinion the two best alive right now, finally on screen together. 

The Heat 2 book was great, would make for an amazing movie but have no idea how they can actually pull off this movie SEQUEL 30+ years later. I’m hesitant, I’m cautious, but yes, absolutely I’m excited! 

Let’s go!
5. Big if huge

## 3. Who’s your favorite actor that can play legendary heroes, crazy villains, biopic characters, and comedy equally well? My pick Russell Crowe

**Score:** 387

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|SnioCkL9cd3B6)

And Heath Ledger
2. Gary Oldman. He can literally play everyone.
3. ![gif](giphy|ABy0eZxpoOrA56WAd9)
4. Tom Hardy
5. Leonardo DiCaprio


(Titanic / Django / Aviator / Catch me if you can)
6. ![gif](giphy|dMyGvQL9W7gvS)
7. Russell Crowe
  - He’s twice the actor he used to be.
8. Love him and Guy Pierce in LA Confidential.
9. He’s not the greatest actor of all time, but Brad Pitt is very good at all 4. So is Johnny Depp.

## 4. I absolutely loved Event Horizon (1997). What are your thoughts on the movie?

**Score:** 303
**Body:** ​The movie has genuinely creepy moments, and many of them even involve gore. The overall plot is also interesting. I remember watching it with my dad, and we both loved it. It was actually a good attempt at sci-fi horror in the 90s. 

I don’t know why it has such a low Rotten Tomatoes score.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. fuck this ship, We're leaving
  - One of the only characters in a horror film to actually be smart from the jump.
  - And he noped out when he saw only one creepy thing in the ship.
2. Horrifying! The only movie that truly gave me nightmares
3. A movie that has aged well! It’s very Hellraiser in space (minus the actual Hellraiser that hand a space movie). It has a great atmosphere and good cast
  - Crew from Hellraiser were involved in EH.
4. I have a funny story about this. Watching the movie in the theater, right when Sam Neil says "let me show you what hell looks like". The power went out. 

Everyone sat in silence for about 2 minutes. Then someone screamed "sure is dark in hell". 

It took about 2-5 minutes before everyone realized the power went out, and it wasn't part of the movie.
5. You mean Warhammer 40k Prequel?
6. It was butchered after the first viewing with the studio forcing changes before the theatrical release. The cut footage was lost and the masters were damaged during storage. We'll never get a Directors Cut or see what the full vision was.
  - May be a controversial take (and of course id still love to see what the cut content was like), but I think the movie benefits from the audience not seeing that much of the horror. The snippets of the hell orgy are so viscerally horrifying and leave you feeling like you just saw a brief fragment of something you were never supposed to see, and can only imagine what absolute terrors went on in that place. I think it adds to the mysterious vibe of the ship and fits well thematically as the characters have traveled beyond where anyone should have traveled and have uncovered some disturbing, unknowable hell. 

## 5. This is exactly what is missing nowadays and what made Old Trek awesome.

**Score:** 246

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Amazing what unlimited free energy can do for a place. 
  - UFO crash Retrievals.
  - No. It wasn't that. Even in-universe it was protest and class warfare.
2. Yes, but it took a looong time to get from here to there… (Also, according to the lore, it will get a lot worse before it gets any better.)
3. This Humanist vision of the future was probably the most unrealistic part of the show.
  - Well, humanity had to be on the brink of self destruction to achieve that. So give us another year, or two.
  - Our future will be more *The Expanse* than *Star Trek*.
4. “The line must be drawn HERE! No farther!! And I will make them PAY for what they’ve done!!!”
5. What happened between TOS and TNG? There were plenty of people looking for wealth when Kirk was captain.
6. There is a reason it was Sci/Fi.....

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Jean-Pierre Jeunet

**Score:** 116
**Body:** If you're a millennial cinephile, you're probably old enough to remember a time when Jean-Pierre Jeunet was considered a filmmaker to watch. Someone spoken of in the same breath as Guillermo del Toro: a visual stylist who brought a quirky, surreal flavor to his films.

He wrote and directed his first two films (*Delicatessen* and *The City of Lost Children*) in collaboration with Marc Caro; Caro would work as a storyboard and conceptual artist on *Alien Resurrection*, Jeunet's only American film. 

Afterwards, he returned to France to cowrite and direct *Amélie*, a critical and commercial smash hit that became the all-time high grossing French film and received 5 Oscar nominations.

It's odd... you don't see many people talking about *Amélie* now, but it was a huge, hugely beloved movie in 2000s-early 2010s cinephile circles. *The New York Times* did name it the 41st greatest film of the 21st century so far, which is a fair ranking in my opinion. 

After *Amélie*, Jean-Pierre Jeunet directed a series of commercial disappointments with mix reviews. I've never seen *Micmacs*, but I know that it does have a cult following. If you've seen it, would you recommend it?

Jeanet has a new movie out later this year, an adaptation of the bestselling French novel *Changer l'eau des fleurs* by Valérie Perrin. Because of that, now seems like as good a time as any to consider the legacy of this filmmaker that seemingly no one ever talks about anymore.

Honestly, I wish that he'd gotten another shot at Hollywood post-*Amélie. Alien Resurrection* is a pretty mediocre movie, but how many franchise part 4s aren't? It's not a debacle, and it actually about broke even, financially.

But he started his career with two pretty interesting, unique films, and you could do a lot worse, legacy-wise, than being remembered as the director of *Amélie.*

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jeunet believes that Harvey Weinstein didn't like that he was so successful without his support and sabotaged both Amelie's chances of winning (it was highly nominated) and his post-Amelie career. [Here's him talking about it in an Independent interview.](https://www.independent.co.uk/arts-entertainment/films/features/jean-pierre-jeunet-bigbug-amelie-b2016305.html) If true, it would make sense why he's getting a second look now. 
  - If that's true, that's very unfortunate. 
  - He did the same to James Gray after not getting along during the making of The Yards. Took Gray almost a decade to make another film and even then, Harvey sabotaged the release of The Immigrant out of spite.
    - Harvey Weinstein was a hateful prick when he had clout. He deserves everything he is getting.
2. I have him in the same category as Tim Burton and Terry Gilliam — an amazing visual stylist who eventually ran aground?  Someone whose classics still hold me in thrall, but where I have trouble getting excited about their next project.

I think Amelie gets talked about a decent bit?  It's still out there, finding its people.  Non-English films are always going to have a smaller audience in America than films in English, but it’s still a perennial on those lists that you mentioned.  It’s in the absolute top tier of crossover hits of that era, with Crouching Tiger Hidden Dragon and Pan’s Labyrinth, and I see all three of those come up pretty regularly.  Over on r/MovieSuggestions, about every other month someone posts “I just saw this amazing film Amelie and I’m looking for more films like that!” and I sigh to myself: no, there really aren’t any more films quite like that.

I thought MicMacs was pretty forgettable.  But right after Amelie came A Very Long Engagement, which I think is quite a strong film, and did better with critics than just “mixed reviews”.  That’s the hidden Jeunet gem I think gets slept on.
  - I loved A Very Long Engagement. I still think about it whenever I make honey on bread for my kids.
  - I thought A Very Long Engagement was so underrated. I personally preferred it to Amelie.
3. Man I love Delicatessen. And City of Lost Children. And there were a few years in the 90s when I told people Jeunet was my favorite filmmaker. I'd love to see him make another movie even close to as unique and interesting as those first two films of his. 

And I liked what he did with Alien too, even if most people didn't.  I thought it was real cool that he brought Dominique Pinon along for the ride.
4. Nice write-up! Those first two films were amazing and so was Amelie. Many years later I watched Micmacs… and it was good! 👍 A fun movie but not up there with the others. Caro made a weird sci-fi film on his own that was… interesting?! Wanna watch delicatessen now!
5. I love JPJ, even his last movie (BigBug) I enjoyed, it wasn’t his best and felt scattered but it has his humour which I really enjoy. I think MicMacs is a little bit slept on. It has his traditional blend of sentimental whimsy but he absolutely nails the “group of misfits coming together to save the day” trope, and that’s one of my favourite movie tropes.

Didn’t know he had a new movie coming, thanks for the heads up. I’ll be adding that to my watchlist. Thank you

## 2. Revisiting Eraserhead

**Score:** 21
**Body:** It's been a long time since I saw Lynch's Eraserhead, his first big film. I was a bit younger then and a lot fresher to film and its more artistic side. Not sure why I watched it again but it's so interesting to see how different films, or any art, is when time has passed. Anytime we watch a film again after years, it can be shaped by our life experiences since then, morphs the film.

In particular, this viewing I could so clearly see Lynch as the visual artist, the painter. Sound is constant throughout, almost subliminal, creating that soundscape of a lost, dark world. Everything is claustrophobic and close, dark and gray. Strange angles and shapes. Not really human. Light is scarce. He makes compositions, pipes leading through the frame, doorways and windows like an exhibition. It has this tangible, tactile feel. You can see the film as if it were on a Lynch canvas, swipes of paint across it, texture. It's a whole world contained in just a few scenes in a tiny neighborhood. Once you enter you're in it, in this dense, stifling world.

That's what particularly stood out this time. The whole feel of the film as this anxiety-ridden film. It's seeping through every scene, dripping. Every movement a struggle, every step, every communication with another human being. It's completely suffused with it, as if the characters are just reaching to the surface, just. Dread clawing out, the film under a heavy veil. Then a baby, well, a sort of baby. Everything revolves around it. No one wants it, but it cries forever, no one can get away. Henry stuck in his room forever, drifting away in dreams. But it has light too. A woman in the radiator. Songs of light. Just because Lynch's films can be drenched in this darkness, he always lets a bit of light through. The beauty of his films come more in its art, it's canvas of sight and sound, this drifting in a lost world of dream and nightmare and floating away

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's funny you say that about coming back to things older with new eyes, because my experience was quite different.


Watching it as a high schooler, I was enchanted by the dark and grimy visual palette of the movie and tantalized by the symbolism.


Coming back to it as a dad, and someone who once had to help an old man calling for help because he fell in a pile of trash on a Philadelphia sidewalk while people walked by, I'm struck by how much Lynch wanted to say that being a parent is hard sometimes and Philly fuckin' sucks.
2. > Just because Lynch's films can be drenched in this darkness, he always lets a bit of light through.

Why do you think the last shot of his first feature film is a complete washout?  He knew this in his twenties--that "we must supply our own light."

He talks in *The Art Life* about how his father and brother kind of cornered him and were like, dude, you've been working on this for years.  You have a wife and a child, you gotta drop this and get real.  This film for David *was* life, I feel like.  It was his beacon and I think it comes through in the film when you watch it.
3. I saw Eraserhead in college.  I took a date and partway through the movie I noticed that she was getting really upset. I eventually asked her what was wrong and she didn’t know, but she wanted to leave.  Outside the theater she basically said that felt stupid because she didn’t understand what the movie was “about”.  I really didn’t know what to say.  

I married her and asked her recently about this, and she didn’t remember this at all.  I really need to rewatch it, because I never saw it all the way through, lol.  


## 3. Has anyone read Scream with Me: Horror Films and the Rise of American Feminism (1968-1980)

**Score:** 9
**Body:** [Eleanor Johnson's book, Scream with Me, takes a look at American feminism and horror films](https://www.thehorrorlounge.com/post/eleanor-johnson-s-book-scream-with-me-takes-a-look-at-american-feminism-and-horror-films)

  
Have anyone read Eleanor Johnson's newish book *Scream with Me*? I thought it was a fairly quick read and less dense than some other academic writing, but she has some good film criticism and insight on the book, especially of *The Shining*, R*osemary's Baby*, and *The Omen*. I'm hoping she does a follow-up to this at some point, focusing on 21st Century horror movies, especially since so much has changed in the last 50 years.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Repulsion is one of the most feminist films I think I've ever seen. It's surprising considering the director, but it genuinely seems to be very feminist. of course Rosemary's Baby is the more refined movie in many ways, but Repulsion has a pure feel to it I can't explain...
  - I agree about Repulsion. I will say that the author addresses the quandary of Roman Polanski, how he made films like Rosemary's Baby and Repulsion, which both have deeply feminist themes, but then, well, we all know what he did. It circles back to that question as to whether or not we can separate the art from the artist.

## 4. Anyone a major fan of Hong Sang-Soo?

**Score:** 6
**Body:** His towering work, On the Beach At Night Alone, was a major disappointment to me. Broken and fragmented performances that didn't pull in any interest. Although, I heavily enjoyed his In Front of Your Face. Simple, disarming and gets straight down to the point without hitting you over the head with it. Bleak, without ever feeling that it's sucking you into it's "pessimism". 

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dude's one of the living masters imo. I like how sometimes a protagonist in one film is a side character in his next, and can be seen casually walking in the background. Combining those layers with his prolific output, and how fascinating each film is on their own right, when you zoom out and look at his output as a retrospective whole, his overall achievement is pretty towering. I can only imagine he'll be viewed as one of the best directors of our generation by most film critics at some point, if they don't already think so. 

Still have yet to see On the Beach At Night Alone though, so can't speak to that film.
2. He’s my favorite director of all time. The way he works in the same style and (broadly speaking) same general themes but always finds a new approach to the material is absolutely brilliant. My favorites are Right Now Wrong Then, The Day He Arrives, Our Sunhi, In Water, and What Does That Nature Say To You.

## 5. Does anybody know of a way to watch Eric Rohmer’s “Pauline At The Beach” online?

**Score:** 7
**Body:** Hi. Hoping this an okay post to make! I’ve been slowly making my way through Rohmer’s filmography in chronological order and i’m up to this movie from 1983. However, it’s nearly impossible to find a way to stream or purchase this with English subtitles :/ Maybe i’m not looking hard enough, but if anyone knows of a way to view/rent this it would be greatly appreciated! EDIT: I’m located in the U.S.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Libraries might be worth a shot, both for DVDs and their various streaming options. I say this because I got into Rohmer last year and found Pauline at the Beach and A Good Marriage at my library.
2. I watch all my movies on free streamers. There are many of them, but I’m not sure if it’s allowed to mention them here. Anyway, you can easily find them (Flyx, cataz, m4uhd, that Russian site, etc.) with English subtitles. (As well as any other movie ever made!)

Down with Netflix, Disney, and all the other corporate bloodsuckers.

---
